text: The colloanal sleeve operation is a workhorse to get out of trouble in the pelvis. If you have a stapler misfire low in the pelvis, then converting this to an endoanal colloanal anastomosis can get you out of trouble. When there is a patient who has a previously failed anastomosis with a pelvic abscess, pelvis is hostile, and sometimes the safest place to be is within the intestinal lumen. The pull-through operation can be used in those circumstances. It can be used with a fistula to the vagina or perineum, or in the circumstances of a rectourethral fistula. The important steps are to have a thoroughly mobilized left colon. The rectum is dissected down to the point of maximum difficulty and then divided across at that level. A mucozectomy is done from above and below to the dentate line. The splenic flexure is mobilized, the colon running on the marginal artery. The colon is then passed through the mucosectomized rectum to the anal canal, where it is anastomosed by hand. A loop ileostomy is constructed at the end.

The patient is a seventy-eight-year-old man who in the past had had a radical prostatectomy for prostate cancer. Subsequently, he developed a rectal cancer for which he received chemoradiation and then a low anterior resection. Regrettably, he then developed a fistula from the anastomosis to the previous radical prostatectomy site as a rectourethral fistula. The planned operation is Suave's colloanal pull-through anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a full-length laparotomy, mobilizing the splenic flexure and running the colon along the marginal artery, ensuring ample reach in the left colon. The operation progresses deep into the pelvis, utilizing a retractor to expose the surgical field. The fistula connecting to the prostatic cavity is clearly visible.

The surgeon raises a mucosal bleb using dilute adrenaline and disconnects the dentate line with scissors, initiating the mucozectomy. The internal sphincter remains intact as the mucozectomy extends to the divided rectal segment, with strips of mucosa being removed. The fistula track and cavity are curetted. The colon is delivered through the anus, and orientation is confirmed before opening the lumen, ensuring no twist in the mesentery.

The surgeon begins the anastomosis with the colon hanging outside the anal canal, placing the first four sutures with difficulty but ensuring a tension-free result. The prolapsing colon is reduced, and sutures are tied to create a tension-free anastomosis. The Eisenhammer retractor is used to view the anal canal, with the needle reversed and rotated for easier transanal suturing. An assistant aids by passing the needle kephalad before withdrawing it from the anus. The anal dilatation time is minimized, occurring only intermittently when the Eisenhammer retractor is in use.

The surgeon checks the anastomosis and inserts additional sutures as needed. Stay sutures are removed, returning the anus to its normal configuration. The colon is hung outside the anus before being reduced to ensure a tension-free anastomosis.
